texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolite T membranes were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis on porous mullite tubes seeded with zeolite T crystals, using milk-like aluminosilicate gel with a molar composition of SiO2:Al2O3:Na2O:K2O:H2O ~ 1:0.05:0.26:0.09:14. A zeolite T crystal layer of about 20 m m in thickness was formed on the outer surface of the support after the synthesis at 373 K for 30 h. Single-gas and mixed-gas permeation experiments through zeolite T membranes were carried out by a vacuum method at 303 y 473 K using He, H2, CO2, O2, N2, CH4, C2H6 and C3H8 single-component gases and CO2/N2, CO2/CH4 and other CO2/hydrocarbon mixtures, respectively. In single-gas permeation experiments, with increasing kinetic diameter from 0.33 nm for CO2 to 0.43 nm for C3H8, the gas permeance decreased by four orders in magnitude, indicating a kind of molecular sieving behavior for the zeolite T membranes prepared in this study, that is, they had little defects and their permeation behavior was controlled by zeolitic pores of erionite. Permeance of CO2 was much higher than those of N2 and CH4 and the ideal selectivities for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 were 31 and 266 at 343 K, respectively. In mixed-gas permeation experiments, zeolite T membranes showed the high selectivities for CO2/ N2 and CO2/CH4 pairs of 107 and 400, respectively, at 308 K. The selectivity a decreased with an increase in temperature, but was still in a high level of 20 and 52 for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4, respectively, even at 473 K. This is due to the synergetic effects of competitive adsorption of CO2 and molecular sieving of zeolitic pores. Because of the increasing effect of single file diffusion, the selectivities for CO2/C2H6 ( a ~ 61) and CO2/C3H8 ( a ~ 17) were rather low.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation and recovery of CO2 from flue gas and natural gas are of great interest from the viewpoint of global warming and energy industries. Membrane separation is energy-efficient and many membranes have been developed for CO2 separation. Among the polymeric membranes developed so far, polyimides and poly(ethylene oxide) segmented block copolyimides have relatively high CO2 separation performances, but also have disadvantages of lower selectivity at higher temperatures and plasticization effect of high CO2 pressure and of coexisting CH4 and other hydrocarbon vapors. 1-3 Inorganic membranes, especially zeolite membranes, have potential of breakthrough to these disadvantages. The affinity between zeolite pore walls and CO2 molecules also plays an important role in separation of CO2 gas mixtures. So far, most of the studies on CO2 separation have been focused on FAU-, MFI-type, and SAPO34 zeolite membranes. Zeolite Y membranes displayed high CO2/N2 separation performances due to preferential adsorption of CO2. 4-6 However, they lost their selectivity rapidly with increasing temperature. 6 Furthermore, the selectivity of CO2 over CH4 was rather low. These are probably because zeolite Y has a large pore size of 0.74 nm and separation by differences in molecular size is not expected to work for these gas pairs. MFI zeolite membranes with a medium pore size of 0.55 nm also displayed very low selectivity for these gas pairs. 7-9 SAPO-34 zeolite membranes of which pore size was estimated to be between 0.43 and 0.50 nm from adsorption experiments, showed molecular-size dependent gas permeation, namely, the single-component gas permeances were in the order of CO2 w N2 w CH4. However, their selectivities of CO2 over N2 and CH4 were still low. 10,11

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolite T with a Si/Al ratio of 3-4 is less hydrophilic than zeolite Y with a Si/Al ratio of less than 3. Zeolite T is an intergrowth-type zeolite of erionite and offretite, of which the pore sizes are 0.36 nm 6 0.51 nm and 0.67 nm 6 0.68 nm, respectively. Intergrowths of the two zeolites causing stacking faults in the building structure are commonly observed. 12-16 Adsorption experiments of hydrocarbon vapors indicated that the large pore openings of offretite were effectively blocked with the small amounts of stacking faults of erionite sheets in the framework. 17 This suggests the effective pore size of zeolite T for permeation is the pore size of erionite or 0.36 nm 6 0.51 nm. CO2 molecules with a kinetic diameter ( s ) of 0.33 nm may permeate through zeolite T membrane without some difficulty, but N2 and CH4 molecules with s s of 0.364 and 0.38 nm, respectively, may permeate with difficulty. Therefore, it is of great interest to investigate the separation of CO2 from N2 and CH4 through zeolite T membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolite T has both hydrophilic and fairly high acid resistant properties, because of their proper Si/Al ratio. We first reported the preparation of zeolite T membranes using a clear aluminosilicate solution with colloidal silica as a silica source. 18 The zeolite T membranes synthesized at 373 K for 24 h showed high pervaporation (PV) performance for water/ organic mixtures.

texts.orig: For practical applications, colloidal silica should be replaced with a less expensive silica source. Recently, we successfully prepared high quality zeolite T membranes by using precipitated silica as the silica source. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aluminosilicate gel was prepared by mixing precipitated silica, sodium aluminate, sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide and distilled water. The mixture was stirred at 303 K for 24 h to prepare a milk-like aluminosilicate gel. The molar composition was SiO2:Al2O3:Na2O:K2O:H2O ~ 1:0.05:0.26: 0.09:14. The outer surface of a porous tubular mullite support (Nikkato Corp., 12 mm in outer diameter, 1.5 mm in thickness, 1.3 m m in average pore size) was polished with SiC paper (# 600), cleaned thoroughly with water and then simply rubbed with a water slurry of zeolite T crystal powder. The seeded support tube of 10 cm in length was placed in the 230 ml aluminosilicate gel and the hydrothermal synthesis was carried out at 373 K for 30 h. Thus, zeolite T membranes were grown hydrothermally on the outer surface of the support tubes. After the hydrothermal synthesis, the membranes were washed completely with water and dried at 348 K for 3 h.
texts.text: The aluminosilicate gel was prepared by mixing precipitated silica, sodium aluminate, sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide and distilled water. The mixture was stirred at 303 K for 24 h to prepare a milk-like aluminosilicate gel. The molar composition was SiO2:Al2O3:Na2O:K2O:H2O ~ 1:0.05:0.26: 0.09:14. The outer surface of a porous tubular mullite support (Nikkato Corp., 12 mm in outer diameter, 1.5 mm in thickness, 1.3 m m in average pore size) was polished with SiC paper (# 600), cleaned thoroughly with water and then simply rubbed with a water slurry of zeolite T crystal powder. The seeded support tube of 10 cm in length was placed in the 230 ml aluminosilicate gel and the hydrothermal synthesis was carried out at 373 K for 30 h. Thus, zeolite T membranes were grown hydrothermally on the outer surface of the support tubes. After the hydrothermal synthesis, the membranes were washed completely with water and dried at 348 K for 3 h.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of mixed gas permeation experiments, the system was evacuated for 1 h and then only the upstream side was cut off from the vacuum line and the mixed-component feed gas was introduced into the upstream side. After 20-30 min, the downstream was cut off from the vacuum line and its pressure was monitored with a pressure transducer. After the permeate pressure reached up to 5 Torr, the permeate in the reservoir was introduced into a gas sampler evacuated beforehand, via a six directional cock, to measure its composition. Then, the downstream side was evacuated again for 20 min and the procedure mentioned above was repeated. The variation in gas permeance of each component and the separation factor with elapsed time were measured for CO2/N2 and CO2/hydrocarbon systems. Permeate composition was analyzed by gas chromatography and the separation factor a A/B was calculated from eqn. (1).

The variation in gas permeance of each component and the separation factor with elapsed time were measured for CO2/N2 and CO2/hydrocarbon systems. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane performances were checked by PV and singlegas permeation. The zeolite T membranes used in this study are listed in Table 1. Their PV performances varied somewhat, that is, the flux ranged from 0.75 to 1.11 kg m 2 2 h 2 1 , and the separation factor ranged from 630 to 1800. The membranes, which were degassed in vacuo over night at 473 K, were used for gas permeation tests. The CO2, N2 and CH4 permeances were (2.2-2.9) 6 10 2 8 , (1.6-2.6) 6 10 2 9 and (2.0-3.0) 6 10 2 10 mol m 2 2 s 2 1 Pa 2 1 , respectively. These permeation properties were reproducible. Membrane T1 was subjected to XRD, FE-SEM analysis. The mixed-gas permeation experiments were performed using membranes T4 and T5.

For membrane T4, the time lags of CH4 and C2H6 were 1.5 min and 9 min, respectively, at 308 K, and 50 s and 4 min, respectively, at 378 K. In order to confirm the steady state permeation, the single-component gas was continuously fed into the upstream side for a long span of 20 h and the downstream side was intermittently evacuated to keep its pressure less than 5 Torr. During the interruption of evacuation, the permeation measurements were repeated. Fig. 4 shows permeances, R s, of CO2, N2 and CH4 as a function of elapsed time for membrane T4. texts.orig: The permeation experiments of C3H8 through membrane T4 were carried out over short and long spans of 5 h and 30 h, respectively. The R value apparently observed was 2 6 10 2 12 mol m 2 2 s 2 1 Pa 2 1 at 378 K, which was a half of the blank leak level of the measurement apparatus of 4 6 10 2 12 mol m 2 2 s 2 1 Pa 2 1 at 378 K, but the time-lag-like behavior was not observed over the experimental spans. texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows adsorption isotherms of CO2, N2, CH4 and C3H8 on zeolite T powder at 298 K, which are Langmuir-type. The adsorptions were much larger for CO2 than for the other gases. It is well known that the adsorption of CO2 depends on the Si/Al ratio of zeolite. 22-25 Zeolite T with a Si/Al ratio of 3-4 has a relatively high content of Al, and therefore a large cationic density, resulting in a large adsorption of CO2. 25 Thus, the high permeance of CO2 for zeolite T membranes is due to both the high adsorption coverage of CO2 in zeolitic pore wall and the relatively easy diffusion of CO2 molecules through the zeolitic pore channels.

On the other hand, R CH4 and R C2H6 showed positive activation energies of 4 and 14 kJ mol 2 1 , respectively, which are relatively small but indicate the importance of activated diffusion for these gases. It is noted that the methane permeances were 10 times and 5 times less than nitrogen ones in zeolite T membranes at 308 K and 473 K, respectively, in spite of the much larger adsorption of methane. SAPO-34 zeolite membranes have been reported to be less permeable to methane than to nitrogen, but their ideal selectivities of N2/CH4 are 3-4 times less than those for zeolite T membranes. 10 For other zeolite membranes with larger zeolitic pores, the methane permeances are similar to or slightly larger than the nitrogen ones. 8,9 Therefore, the presence of high N2/CH4 selectivity is clear evidence for molecular sieving contribution in zeolite T membranes.

texts.orig: permeance with an increase in the pressure, as shown by the dotted lines in Figs. 9 and 10, which are the calculated lines of the feed pressure dependence of single gas premeance of CO2, N2 and CH4 based on Langmuir type adsorption. However, the present results indicated that with an increase in the feed pressure the single gas permeance of CO2 did not decreased so much as considered from the saturation of adsorption and the single gas permeance of N2, CH4, C2H6 and C3H8 hardly decreased or rather increased. In some cases, this kind of pressure dependence of single gas permeance through a zeolite membrane can be attributed to the overlap of the viscous flow and Knudsen diffusion contributions through defects or nonzeolitic pores to the surface diffusion contribution through zeolitic pores. 7 However, this is not the present case. A probable level of contribution of the transport through nonzeolitic pores can explain the feed pressure dependence of permeance of a gas (say N2), but is too low to explain that of the second gas (CO2) and is too high for the third gas (CH4). Therefore, the above-mentioned result suggests the presence of some effects enhancing the transport of gas molecules in zeolitic pore channels of Zeolite T at higher feed pressures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the CO2/N2 system, the competitive adsorption is very effective and significantly reduces the N2 adsorption, whereas the molecular sieving effect is not so strict and the single file diffusion effect is slightly operative because of both the relatively easy diffusion and low loading of N2 in zeolitic pores. In the CO2/CH4 system, the competitive adsorption is effective, whereas the molecular sieving becomes more effective and the single file diffusion effect becomes strict but not so serious. The competitive adsorption effect and the single file diffusion effect cancelled out as for the mixed CH4 permeance and the single file diffusion effect does not seriously decrease the mixed CO2 permeance, and as a result zeolite T membrane keeps the high mixed CO2 permeance with the high mixture selectivity of CO2/CH4. In the CO2/C2H6 system, the competitive adsorption becomes less effective, whereas the molecular sieving becomes much more effective and the single file diffusion effect becomes stricter and very serious, because of both the very difficult diffusion and a relatively high loading of C2H6 in zeolitic pores. The single file diffusion effect plays a rather important role in the mixed-component permeation, resulting in a seriously large decrease in the mixed CO2 permeance and a slight increase in the mixed C2H6 permeance. Consequently, zeolite T membrane showed a rather low mixed CO2 permeance with a lower mixture selectivity of CO2/C2H6.

texts.orig: Fig. 16 shows permeance and selectivity for equimolar mixture of CO2/C3H8 as a function of elapsed time at 378 K. Both the CO2 permeance and the mixture selectivity of CO2/C3H8 decreased significantly during the initial period of about 2 h. This is clearly due to the single file diffusion effect. During this initial period, adsorption of C3H8 in zeolitic pores proceeded. Although the steady-state permeation was attained after about 4 h, most of the change in permeance occurred within 2 h. This is reasonable judging from the time-lag for single-component permeation of C3H8 of 40 min.

texts.orig: Table 3 shows the comparison of CO2 separation performances of zeolite membranes for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 systems. These membranes were evaluated with a sweep gas or vacuum method. The common feature of these two experimental methods is the permeate pressure close to zero. Even if the difference in experimental methods is taken into consideration, the following are reasonably deduced. For CO2/N2 separation, the zeolite T membranes are comparable to FAU-type (Y-Na) membrane with the high selectivity, 5,6 and much better than the SAPO-34 membranes. 10 For CO2/CH4 separation, the zeolite T membranes showed the excellent performance compared with other zeolite membranes. The separation in zeolite Y membranes is based only on the preferential adsorption of CO2 because of their large zeolitic pores (0.74 nm). Therefore, the zeolite Y membranes displayed higher CO2/N2 separation performances at a lower temperature but lost their selectivity rapidly with increasing temperature. Furthermore, they

texts.orig: displayed rather low selectivity of CO2/CH4. MFI zeolite membranes with a medium pore size of 0.55 nm also displayed very low selectivity of these gas pairs. Although having a smaller pore size of between 0.43 and 0.50 nm, the SAPO-34 zeolite membranes showed the rather low selectivity of CO2 over N2 and CH4, indicating that the synergetic effects of competitive adsorption and molecular sieving was not enough high for CO2 separations. In contrast with these membranes, zeolite T membranes with Si/Al ratio of 3-4 and the effective pore size of 0.36 6 0.51 nm, showed very high CO2 separation performances. As for CO2/CH4 separation, the a value of 400 at 308 K for zeolite T membrane (T4) is much higher than those for other membranes. Furthermore, it is noted that the zeolite T membrane retained the high selectivity of a ~ 52 and 20 for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2, respectively, even at 473 K. This is the first case that the zeolite membrane has the high selectivity for CO2 separation mainly due to the molecular sieving effect.

texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (1) Zeolite T membranes synthesized on tubular porous mullite tubes by hydrothermal synthesis displayed molecular sieving behavior in single-component permeation, namely, with increasing kinetic diameter of gas molecule from 0.33 nm for CO2 to 0.43 nm for C3H8 the gas permeance decreased by more than 3 orders in magnitude.
texts.text: (1) Zeolite T membranes synthesized on tubular porous mullite tubes by hydrothermal synthesis displayed molecular sieving behavior in single-component permeation, namely, with increasing kinetic diameter of gas molecule from 0.33 nm for CO2 to 0.43 nm for C3H8 the gas permeance decreased by more than 3 orders in magnitude.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (2) In mixed-gas permeation, the membranes selectively permeated carbon dioxide from CO2/N2 mixture and CO2/ hydrocarbon mixtures with high separation performances, which were due to synergetic effects of competitive adsorption of CO2 and molecular sieving. Zeolite T membrane showed very high selectivities of a ~ 107 and 400 for CO2/N2 and CO2/ CH4, respectively, at 308K and a ~ 20 and 52, respectively, even at 473 K.
texts.text: (2) In mixed-gas permeation, the membranes selectively permeated carbon dioxide from CO2/N2 mixture and CO2/ hydrocarbon mixtures with high separation performances, which were due to synergetic effects of competitive adsorption of CO2 and molecular sieving. Zeolite T membrane showed very high selectivities of a ~ 107 and 400 for CO2/N2 and CO2/ CH4, respectively, at 308K and a ~ 20 and 52, respectively, even at 473 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (3) Single-file diffusion effect became more significant for binary mixtures of CO2 and hydrocarbon with an increase in carbon number. As a result, zeolite T membranes showed the rather low selectivities of a ~ 61 and 17 for CO2/C2H6 and CO2/C3H8, respectively, at 308 K.
texts.text: (3) Single-file diffusion effect became more significant for binary mixtures of CO2 and hydrocarbon with an increase in carbon number. As a result, zeolite T membranes showed the rather low selectivities of a ~ 61 and 17 for CO2/C2H6 and CO2/C3H8, respectively, at 308 K.
